content:
# Leviticus Chapter 11 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 63v–64r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_011_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_011_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 95–96 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_011_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_011_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 11 is the foundational chapter of the Levitical purity legislation (chs. 11–15), and the longest single chapter on the subject. It treats the question of which creatures may be eaten and which defile. The chapter divides into five major sections by creature-type, followed by a theological summary: (1) vv.1–8: land animals — the dual criterion of split hoof AND cud-chewing, with four case-study animals that fulfill only one criterion; (2) vv.9–12: water creatures — the dual criterion of fins AND scales; (3) vv.13–19: birds — listed by prohibition, with no positive criterion stated; (4) vv.20–23: flying insects — prohibition with exception for jointed-legged jumping insects; (5) vv.24–40: the defilement of carcass-contact, with graduated rules for corpse-touching and vessel-contamination; (6) vv.41–45: swarming creatures on the ground — prohibited in all forms; (7) vv.46–47: summary formula. The theological keystone is the double injunction of vv.44–45: "be holy, for I am holy" (qedu-shim tihyu ki qadosh ani) — the only place in the entire Levitical purity legislation where the dietary laws are grounded explicitly in the divine holiness. The chapter is addressed to both Moshe and Aharon (v.1), the only chapter in the book so far addressed to both simultaneously outside ch.13 and 14. MT and English versification are identical throughout.]*

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moshe [YHWH el-Moshe] and to Aharon [ve-el-Aharon] [ve-el-Aharon — "and to Aharon"; uniquely, this chapter is addressed to both Moshe and Aharon together, not to Moshe alone; the dual address signals that the dietary laws are a matter of priestly instruction (Aharon as teaching-priest, cf. 10:11) as well as Mosaic legislation; later, Lev 13–14 will similarly be addressed to both; the combination may reflect that the distinction-making function of chs. 11–15 belongs especially to the priestly office — it is the priest who must teach Israel to distinguish clean from unclean (10:10–11)], saying [lemor] to them [alehem]:

**2** "Speak to the sons of Yisra'el [dabru el-bney Yisra'el] saying [lemor]: These [are] the living-creatures [ha-chayyah] [ha-chayyah — "the living thing / the creature / the animal"; from chayah, "to live"; broadly denotes any living creature, but in the dietary context refers specifically to land mammals; the term is distinguished from 'of (birds, v.13), sherets ha-mayim (water-swarming things, v.10), and sherets ha-'of (flying-swarming things, v.20); the LXX renders ha-chayyah as τὰ κτήνη (ta ktēnē, "the domestic animals / the beasts") — a slight narrowing from the Hebrew's broader chayyah, though context supports the domestic/wild land-animal reference] that you shall eat [asher tokhelu] from among all the animals [min-kol-ha-behemah] [min-kol-ha-behemah — "from all the cattle/animals"; behemah is the broader term for large quadrupeds, both domestic (cattle, sheep, goats) and wild; the structure of the verse moves from the general (ha-chayyah, "the living-creature type") to the specific (min-kol-ha-behemah, "from among all the animal category"), setting up the criteria that follow] that are upon the land [asher al-ha-arets]:

**3** "Every [one that is] a divider-of-the-hoof [kol-mafresset parsah] [kol-mafresset parsah — "every one dividing a hoof"; parsah is the hoof; mafresset is hiphil participle feminine of paras, "to split, divide, spread"; the divided hoof (parsah shessu'ah, "cloven hoof") refers to ungulate feet with a complete division between two toes — as in cattle, sheep, goats, deer; the hiphil participle designates the action as the animal's own characteristic: it "does the splitting"; the LXX renders mafresset parsah as διχηλοῦν ὁπλήν (dichēloun hoplēn, "having a cleft hoof"), using διχηλέω (dichēleō, "to split the hoof into two"), from δίχα (dicha, "in two") + χηλή (chēlē, "hoof/claw") — a precise rendering] and [that is] a shearer-of-the-sheaf [ve-shosa'at shesa'] [ve-shosa'at shesa' — "and splitting a cleavage/crack"; shosa'at is qal participle of shasas, "to split, cleave"; shesa' is the noun "cleft, crack, split" from the same root; this second phrase appears to be a clarifying expansion of the first: the hoof must be fully cleaved/split through — some commentators distinguish mafresset parsah (the outer division visible from above) from shosa'at shesa' (the full cleft that goes through the hoof); the combination ensures the criterion requires a complete division, not merely a partial one; the LXX renders ve-shosa'at shesa' as καὶ ὀνυχιστῆρας ὀνυχιζούσης (kai onychistēras onychizousēs, "and having toenail-like things / claws that claw"), which departs from the MT's sense of a cleft and moves toward nail/claw imagery — a divergence noted by Origen and later commentators] and that brings up the cud [ve-ma'alat gerah] [ve-ma'alat gerah — "and bringing up the cud"; ma'alat is hiphil participle of 'alah, "to go up, to bring up"; gerah is the technical term for the regurgitated material that ruminant animals chew: cud; the combination of the two criteria — split hoof AND cud-chewing — defines the class of edible land mammals in the Levitical system; both criteria must be present; either alone is insufficient; the LXX renders ve-ma'alat gerah as καὶ ἀνάγουσαν μηρυκισμόν (kai anagousan mērykismon, "and bringing up a rumination"), using μηρυκισμός (mērykismos, "rumination / chewing the cud"), derived from μηρυκάζω (mērykázō, "to chew the cud") — a Homeric and biological term that precisely captures the MT's gerah] — among the animals [ba-behemah] — it [you may eat] [the permissive verb "you may eat" is implied in the Hebrew; the MT construction here is a pure participle clause without an explicit verb of permission; the LXX supplies τοῦτο φάγεσθε (touto phagesthe, "this you shall eat"), making explicit what the MT leaves implicit by contrast with the following prohibition formula]:

**4** "Nevertheless [akh] — this [you shall not eat] [akh introduces the exception clause: "but / however / nevertheless"; it signals that what follows qualifies the permission just stated; the implied "you shall not eat" mirrors the implied "you may eat" of v.3, balancing the chiastic structure; the LXX renders akh as πλήν (plēn, "however / but / except"), which precisely captures its adversative force here] from those-that-bring-up-the-cud [min-ha-ma'aleh gerah] and from those-that-divide-the-hoof [u-min-mafrisey ha-parsah]: the camel [et-ha-gamal] [et-ha-gamal — "the camel"; gamal is the dromedary (Camelus dromedarius) and/or the Bactrian camel (Camelus bactrianus), both known in the ancient Near East and used for transport and caravan trade (Gen 24:10; 37:25; Job 1:3); the camel chews cud but does not have a fully divided hoof — its feet are padded, not hoofed; it therefore meets one criterion but not both; the LXX renders gamal as κάμηλον (kamēlon, "camel"), straightforward transliteration-into-Greek of the Semitic root] — for it brings up the cud [ki ma'aleh gerah hu'] but the hoof it does not divide [ve-parsah eynenu mafris] [ve-parsah eynenu mafris — "and a hoof it does not divide / it does not have a split hoof"; the construction eynenu mafris ("there is no dividing-of it" / "it-is-not dividing") is a verbal negation with the personal suffix: literally "its hoof-division it-does-not"; the Hebrew is precise: the camel has the cud-chewing criterion but lacks the hoof-dividing criterion; therefore it is unclean; the LXX: ὁπλὴν δὲ οὐ διχηλεῖ (hoplēn de ou dichēlei, "but it does not split the hoof"), confirming the MT] — it is unclean for you [tame' hu' lakhem] [tame' hu' lakhem — "it is unclean for you"; tame' is the key term of ritual impurity throughout Leviticus — not moral pollution but a ritual status that bars entry to the sacred sphere and conveys contamination through contact; the phrase lakhem ("for you") is consistently added throughout ch.11, signaling that the classification is relational — these animals are tame' not in some abstract ontological sense but in relation to Israel's covenant life before the LORD; the LXX renders tame' consistently as ἀκάθαρτος (akathartos, "unclean, impure"), which becomes the standard NT term for ritual/demonic uncleanness (Mark 1:23-26; Acts 10:14)]:

**5** "And the rock-badger [ve-et-ha-shaphan] [ve-et-ha-shaphan — "and the rock-badger"; shaphan is most likely the hyrax (Procavia capensis) — a small, stout mammal found throughout the Levant, living among rocks (Ps 104:18; Prov 30:26); the hyrax does not truly chew cud but performs a pseudo-rumination (caecotrophy or cecal fermentation) that may have appeared to ancient observers as cud-chewing; it has soft padded feet, not split hooves; it therefore appears to meet one criterion but not the other; older translations rendered shaphan as "coney" (rabbit-like animal), following the LXX and Vulgate; the LXX renders shaphan as χοιρογρύλλιον (choirogyllion, "pig-squealer" or "sow-hedgehog" — a puzzling identification; the term appears to denote a small, hedgehog- or porcupine-like creature; this identification is unlikely to be accurate and may reflect uncertainty about the Hebrew term in the Alexandrian Jewish community; Aquila renders it πύραξ (pyrax)], for it brings up the cud [ki ma'aleh gerah hu'] but the hoof it does not divide [ve-parsah lo' yafris] — it is unclean for you [tame' hu' lakhem]:

**6** "And the hare [ve-et-ha-arnevet] [ve-et-ha-arnevet — "and the hare"; arnevet is the hare (Lepus capensis or related Levantine species); like the hyrax, the hare does not truly ruminate but engages in cecotrophy (re-ingesting cecal pellets) — a practice that to ancient observers resembled cud-chewing; the hare also does not have split hooves; its feet are padded with fur; it appears to meet one criterion but not the other; the LXX renders arnevet as δασύποδα (dasypodan, "hairy-footed one" — literally "rough-footed"), which is a fair description of the hare's padded feet; the Greek term dasypos later becomes the standard Greek word for "hare"] — for it brings up the cud [ki ma'alas gerah hi'] [note the feminine form: the camel (v.4) and rock-badger (v.5) use masculine hu'; the hare uses feminine hi' — this may reflect the Hebrew grammatical gender of arnevet (feminine noun), not an observation about sex of the animal; the LXX does not preserve this gender distinction in Greek] but the hoof it has not divided [ve-parsah lo' hofrisah] — it is unclean for you [teme'ah hi' lakhem]:

**7** "And the pig [ve-et-ha-chazir] [ve-et-ha-chazir — "and the pig"; chazir is the domestic pig (Sus scrofa domestica); the pig is the paradigmatic unclean animal in biblical, post-biblical Jewish, and Islamic tradition; uniquely among the four case-study animals, the pig meets the hoof criterion but not the cud criterion: it has a fully divided hoof but does not chew cud; this makes it distinctive — it appears superficially clean by one criterion while failing the other; several ancient and modern commentators have noted the particular rhetorical weight given to the pig in this list: it is listed last among the four, and its description is slightly expanded (see below); the LXX renders chazir as ὗν (hyn, accusative of hys, "pig/swine")], for it [is] a divider of the hoof [ki mafris parsah hu'] and [a] shearer of the cleft [ve-shosa' shesa' parsah] [ve-shosa' shesa' parsah — "and cleaving a cleavage of the hoof"; the dual hoof-description for the pig is remarkable: both mafris parsah and shosa' shesa' parsah are applied — the most emphatic possible hoof-criterion language; the pig's hoof is thus formally twice-certified as meeting the visual criterion, before the disqualifying criterion is stated; this structure mirrors the rhetorical trap — the pig looks like a clean animal; it has the external mark of cleanness; but it lacks the internal process of cud-chewing; the pig becomes in Jewish tradition and in later NT usage (Matt 7:6; 2 Pet 2:22) the emblem of that which appears holy but is not] but the cud it does not bring up [ve-hu' gerah lo' yiggar] [ve-hu' gerah lo' yiggar — "but it — the cud — does not chew"; the pronoun hu' ("it") stands at the front of the clause for emphasis, separating subject from predicate: "but IT — cud — does not chew"; the LXX: ἐκεῖνος δὲ οὐκ ἀνάγει μηρυκισμόν (ekeinos de ouk anagei mērykismon, "but that one does not bring up rumination"); compare the identical phrasing for camel (v.4) and rock-badger (v.5) — the pig's description is structurally parallel but with the hoof-criterion doubled] — it is unclean for you [tame' hu' lakhem]:

**8** "From their flesh [mi-besaram] you shall not eat [lo' tokhelu] and their carcass [u-ve-nivlatam] [u-ve-nivlatam — "and their carcass / their corpse"; nivlah (from naval, "to be withered / to fall") is the standard term throughout ch.11 for the dead body of an unclean animal; it contrasts with sherets ("swarming thing") and with the "carcass" of a clean animal that died without slaughter (Lev 17:15; Deut 14:21); contact with a nivlah conveys ritual impurity (tum'ah) that must be resolved by washing; the LXX renders nivlatam as τῶν θνησιμαίων αὐτῶν (tōn thēsimaiōn autōn, "of their dead ones / carcasses"), from θνῄσκω (thnēskō, "to die")] you shall not touch [lo' tigg'u] — they are unclean for you [teme'im hem lakhem].

---

**9** "These [you may eat] from all that is in the waters [mi-kol asher ba-mayim] [mi-kol asher ba-mayim — "from all that is in the waters"; the dietary law for water-creatures covers any body of water — seas, rivers, lakes, streams (as confirmed by v.9b: "in the seas and in the rivers"); no distinction is made between saltwater and freshwater; the criterion applies universally to all aquatic creatures]: every [one that has] fins [kol asher lo snapir] [kol asher lo snapir — "every one that has a fin"; snapir (pl. snapirim) is the fin of a fish; the fin enables directed swimming; the LXX renders snapir as πτερύγια (pterygion, "little wings / fins"), from πτέρυξ (pteryx, "wing") — the fin is imaged as an underwater wing; this is a natural metaphorical extension used by both LXX translators and later biological writers] and scales [ve-qasqeset] [ve-qasqeset — "and scales"; qasqeset (pl. qasqasot) are fish scales — the overlapping external plates that cover the skin of most bony fish; the Hebrew term is onomatopoeic in sound (qasqasot); the LXX renders qasqeset as λεπίδας (lepidas, "scales"), from λεπίς (lepis, "scale / husk"), the standard Greek biological term for fish scales; the combination of snapir (fins) and qasqeset (scales) is the dual criterion for edible water-creatures, directly parallel to the dual hoof-and-cud criterion for land animals] — in the seas [ba-yamim] and in the rivers [u-va-nechalim] — them you shall eat [otam tokhelu]:

**10** "And all that has not [fins and scales] [ve-kol asher eyn lo snapir ve-qasqeset] — in the seas [ba-yamim] and in the rivers [u-va-nechalim] — from all the swarmers of the water [mi-kol sherets ha-mayim] [mi-kol sherets ha-mayim — "from all the swarming-things of the waters"; sherets is the key term for the class of small, proliferating, teeming creatures — the verb sharats means "to swarm, to teem" (cf. Gen 1:20-21; 7:21; 8:17; Ex 1:7); sherets ha-mayim encompasses all water creatures that lack fins and scales: shellfish, crustaceans, eels, rays, squid, octopus, cetaceans (whales, dolphins), and similar creatures; the LXX renders sherets ha-mayim as ἀπό παντὸς ὧν ἐὰν μὴ ᾖ αὐτοῖς πτερύγια καὶ λεπίδες τῶν ἐν τοῖς ὕδασιν (apo pantos hōn ean mē ē autois pterygion kai lepidas tōn en tois hydasi — "from everything that does not have fins and scales of those in the waters")] and from all the living-things [u-mi-kol nefesh ha-chayyah] [u-mi-kol nefesh ha-chayyah — "and from all the living-being of the living"; nefesh ha-chayyah ("living soul/being") is the same phrase used of living creatures at Gen 1:20-21, 24; 2:7; the combination of sherets ha-mayim and nefesh ha-chayyah broadens the prohibition to cover every water creature without exception — the two terms together ensure the prohibition is comprehensive, from the smallest invertebrate to the largest aquatic mammal] that is in the waters — they are a detestable-thing [sheqets hem] [sheqets hem — "they are a detestable-thing / an abomination"; sheqets (from shaqats, "to abhor, to detest") is a strong ritual-revulsion term — stronger than the standard tame' ("unclean"); it denotes not merely the ritual status of impurity but an active abhorrence that characterizes the proper Israelite response to these creatures; sheqets appears 11 times in Leviticus 11 alone, making it the defining term of the chapter; the LXX renders sheqets as βδέλυγμα (bdelygma, "abomination / that which causes disgust"), from βδελύσσομαι (bdelyssomai, "to feel nauseous / to be disgusted") — this is the same term used throughout the NT and LXX for moral and idolatrous abominations (Dan 9:27; 11:31; 12:11; Rev 17:4-5; Matt 24:15)] to you [lakhem]:

**11** "And they shall be a detestable-thing to you [ve-sheqets yihyu lakhem] — from their flesh you shall not eat [mi-besaram lo' tokhelu] and their carcasses you shall treat-as-detestable [ve-et-nivlatam teshaqtsu] [ve-et-nivlatam teshaqtsu — "and their carcasses you shall abhor / you shall treat as abominations"; teshaqtsu is piel of shaqats, "to abhor / to make abominable / to treat as detestable"; the piel is causative-factitive: "you shall make-abominable [them]" — i.e., you shall treat them as sheqets; the verb converts the noun sheqets into an active stance: Israel is commanded not merely to avoid these creatures but to cultivate an active revulsion toward them; this internalizing of ritual categories is one of the pedagogical purposes of the dietary laws]:

**12** "Everything that has not fins and scales [kol asher eyn lo snapir ve-qasqeset] in the waters [ba-mayim] — it is a detestable-thing for you [sheqets hu' lakhem]."

---

**13** "And these [are them] that you shall treat-as-detestable [ve-et-eleh teshaqtsu] from the birds [min-ha-'of] [min-ha-'of — "from the birds"; 'of is the general term for birds and flying creatures; unlike the land-animal and water-creature sections, the bird-prohibition section gives no positive criterion — no feature that makes a bird edible is described; instead, a list of twenty prohibited species is given (vv.13-19); the permitted birds are defined negatively: all birds except those on the list; this asymmetry (positive criteria for land and water; negative list for birds) may reflect the difficulty of articulating a simple anatomical criterion that separates permitted from prohibited birds; broadly, the prohibited birds are birds of prey, carrion-eaters, and water-birds — all of which feed on blood, carrion, or creatures that are themselves ritually problematic; the LXX gives a similar list with some divergences in species identification] — they shall not be eaten [lo' ye'akhelu] — they are a detestable-thing [sheqets hem]: the eagle [ha-nesher] [ha-nesher — "the eagle / the vulture"; nesher can denote either the griffon vulture (Gyps fulvus), the most common large raptor of the Levant, or the golden/imperial eagle; most modern zoologists prefer "griffon vulture" for nesher based on behavioral descriptions (Prov 30:17; Mic 1:16); the LXX renders nesher as ἀετόν (aeton, "eagle"), and English versions have traditionally rendered it "eagle" — but "vulture" may be more accurate], and the ossifrage [ve-ha-peres] [ve-ha-peres — "and the ossifrage / bearded vulture / lammergeier"; peres (from paras, "to break") means "breaker" — the bone-breaker; this is almost certainly the lammergeier (Gypaetus barbatus), a large vulture known for dropping bones from great height onto rocks to break them and extract the marrow; the LXX renders peres as γρύπα (grypa, "griffin")], and the osprey [ve-ha-ozniyyah] [ve-ha-ozniyyah — "and the osprey / sea-eagle"; the identification is uncertain; it may be the short-toed eagle (Circaetus gallicus) or the osprey (Pandion haliaetus); LXX: ἁλιαέτον (haliaeton, "sea-eagle"), which supports the osprey identification]:

**14** "And the kite [ve-ha-da'ah] [ve-ha-da'ah — "and the kite"; da'ah (also da'at in some MSS) is likely the black kite (Milvus migrans) or red kite (Milvus milvus) — soaring raptors common throughout the Levant; Job 28:7 mentions the 'ayyah as a keen-sighted bird, and the two terms may be related; LXX: ἰκτῖνα (iktina, "kite")] and the falcon [ve-ha-ayyah] after its kind [le-mineha] [le-mineha — "after its kind"; the phrase le-min / le-mineihu / le-mineha ("after its kind / according to its species") is the same phrase used at Gen 1:11-12, 21, 24-25 for the reproductive categories of creation; its use here signals that the prohibition extends to all species within the given bird-type; it appears with falcon (v.14), raven (v.15), ostrich-group (v.16), hawk (v.16), and others — wherever the Hebrew author is aware that a term covers multiple subspecies; the LXX renders le-mineha as κατὰ γένος αὐτοῦ (kata genos autou, "according to its kind")]:

**15** "Every raven [kol-'orev] after its kind [le-mino] [kol-'orev le-mino — "every raven after its kind"; 'orev is the raven (Corvus corax) and by extension the entire corvid family (ravens, crows, rooks, jackdaws, etc.); the phrase kol-'orev le-mino ("every raven after its kind") makes the prohibition cover all corvid species; LXX: κόρακα (koraka, "raven") with κατὰ γένος αὐτοῦ]:

**16** "And the ostrich [ve-et-bat ha-ya'anah] [ve-et-bat ha-ya'anah — "and the daughter of the desert-howling / the bat-ya'anah"; bat ha-ya'anah literally means "daughter of howling / daughter of the wasteland"; it is the standard identification for the ostrich (Struthio camelus) in biblical Hebrew (Job 30:29; Isa 13:21; 34:13; 43:20; Jer 50:39; Lam 4:3; Mic 1:8); the ostrich is the largest bird and is flightless; it was native to the Levantine desert regions until the 20th century; the LXX renders bat ha-ya'anah as στρουθὸν (strouthon, "sparrow" — but used in Greek for large birds generally, including the ostrich) or, in some manuscripts, νυκτικόρακα (nyktikoraka, "night-raven / screech-owl")], and the nighthawk [ve-ha-tachmas] [ve-ha-tachmas — "and the nighthawk / the barn-owl / the swallow"; tachmas is uncertain; it may derive from chamas ("to do violence"), suggesting a violent or grasping bird; some propose the nighthawk (Caprimulgus europaeus) or the swift; LXX: γλαῦκα (glauka, "owl")], and the seagull [ve-ha-shachaf] [ve-ha-shachaf — "and the seagull / the herring gull"; shachaf from the root meaning "to be thin / to waste away" — perhaps because gulls appear lean; LXX: λάρον (laron, "gull")], and the hawk [ve-ha-nets] after its kind [le-minehu] [ve-ha-nets le-minehu — "and the hawk after its kind"; nets is likely the sparrowhawk (Accipiter nisus) or similar accipiter; the phrase le-minehu extends the prohibition to all hawk species; LXX: ἱέρακα (hieraka, "hawk") κατὰ γένος αὐτοῦ]:

**17** "And the little-owl [ve-ha-kos] [ve-ha-kos — "and the little-owl"; kos (literally "cup / vessel") as a bird name is the little owl (Athene noctua), very common throughout the Levant; Ps 102:6 uses kos as a simile for desolation and loneliness ("I am like an owl of the wasteland"); LXX: νυκτικόρακα (nyktikoraka, "night-raven / owl")], and the cormorant [ve-ha-shalakh] [ve-ha-shalakh — "and the cormorant / the plunging-bird"; shalakh from shalakh ("to throw / to hurl / to cast down") — a bird that plunges for fish; this may be the common cormorant (Phalacrocorax carbo) or the gannet; LXX: καταρράκτην (katarraktēn, "cataract-bird / plunger"), from καταρράσσω (katarrassō, "to dash down") — a fitting rendering of the plunge-diving behavior], and the great-owl [ve-ha-yanshuf] [ve-ha-yanshuf — "and the great-owl"; yanshuf may derive from neshafah ("twilight / blowing / dusk") — a dusk-active bird; it is likely the eagle owl (Bubo bubo) or the tawny owl; LXX: ἴβιν (ibin, "ibis") — a surprising identification; the ibis is an Egyptian sacred bird, not an owl, and the LXX identification here is likely erroneous]:

**18** "And the white-owl [ve-ha-tinshemet] [ve-ha-tinshemet — "and the white-owl / the barn-owl"; tinshemet in the bird-list (as distinct from its appearance in the reptile-list at v.30 where it likely means a different creature) may be the barn-owl (Tyto alba), whose ghostly white appearance and eerie cry are distinctive; from nashaf ("to blow / breathe"), suggesting a bird of the night-breeze; LXX: πορφυρίωνα (porphyriōna, "purple-hen / red-billed coot") — another uncertain identification], and the pelican [ve-ha-qa'at] [ve-ha-qa'at — "and the pelican"; qa'at is most likely the white pelican (Pelecanus onocrotalus) or the dalmatian pelican; Ps 102:6 pairs kos and qa'at in a desolation-image; LXX: πελεκᾶνα (pelekana, "pelican") — accurate identification], and the vulture [ve-ha-racham] [ve-ha-racham — "and the vulture / the Egyptian vulture"; racham is likely the Egyptian vulture (Neophron percnopterus), which was associated with compassion/motherliness (rachamim, "compassion") in popular etymology, though the bird is actually a bone-cracker and carrion feeder; LXX: πορφυρίωνα (porphyriōna) or ῥοδοπτέρυγα (rhodopteryga, "rose-winged") — divergent identifications]:

**19** "And the stork [ve-ha-chasidah] [ve-ha-chasidah — "and the stork"; chasidah literally means "the faithful/loyal one / the kind one," from chesed ("covenant-faithfulness / loyal-love"); the stork was observed to care tenderly for its young and to return faithfully to its nesting sites; despite this folk-etymology suggesting virtue, the stork is prohibited; it is likely the white stork (Ciconia ciconia), a migratory bird that passes through Israel twice yearly; LXX: ἐρωδιόν (erōdion, "heron") — an imprecise identification], the heron [ve-ha-anafah] after its kind [le-mineha] [ve-ha-anafah le-mineha — "and the heron after its kind"; anafah may derive from 'anaph ("to be angry") or from a root meaning "nose/snout"; it is likely the grey heron (Ardea cinerea) or one of the egret species; le-mineha extends the prohibition to all heron relatives; LXX: χαραδριόν (charadrion, "plover / river-bird")], and the hoopoe [ve-ha-duchifat] [ve-ha-duchifat — "and the hoopoe"; duchifat is the hoopoe (Upupa epops), a distinctive crested bird common throughout the Levant; its appearance is unmistakable — orange-cinnamon body, black-and-white wings, fan-shaped crest; it feeds by probing the ground for grubs and is associated with carrion in several ancient sources; it is famous in later Jewish legend as the bird of Shlomo (Solomon); LXX: ἔποπα (epopa, "hoopoe") — accurate identification], and the bat [ve-ha-'atallef] [ve-ha-'atallef — "and the bat"; 'atallef is the bat (order Chiroptera); its inclusion in the bird-list reflects the ancient taxonomic category of 'of as any flying creature, not merely feathered birds; bats are neither birds nor unclean by the mammal-criteria of vv.3-8, but as flying creatures that eat insects (and blood in some species) and emerge at night, they are classified with the prohibited 'of; modern taxonomy places bats with mammals, but the Levitical system classifies by habitat-and-locomotion rather than biological family; LXX: νυκτερίδα (nykterida, "bat") — accurate identification].

---

**20** "Every swarming-thing of the birds [kol sherets ha-'of] [kol sherets ha-'of — "every swarming-thing of the birds / every flying swarmer"; sherets ha-'of is the class of flying insects — creatures that both fly ('of) and swarm (sherets); the category is distinct from the birds of vv.13-19 (which are prohibited by species-list) and from the water-swarmers of v.10 (prohibited without exception); the flying-insect category has a significant exception (vv.21-22): certain jumping insects are permitted] that walks [ha-holek] on four [al-arba] [al-arba — "on four [legs/feet]"; flying insects that have four legs appear to walk on them; in fact, most insects have six legs, but the two rearmost legs of grasshoppers and locusts are the jumping legs and may not have been counted as standard walking legs in the ancient observation system; the Mishnah later distinguishes the four walking legs from the two jumping legs] — it is a detestable-thing to you [sheqets hu' lakhem]:

**21** "Nevertheless [akh] — this you may eat [tokhelu] from every swarming-thing of the birds [mi-kol sherets ha-'of] that walks on four [ha-holek al-arba]: which [asher] has knees above its legs [lo' kera'ayim mi-ma'al le-raglaw] [lo' kera'ayim mi-ma'al le-raglaw — "which has knees/shanks above its feet/legs"; kera'ayim is the dual of kera' ("leg / lower leg / shank"); mi-ma'al ("from above") + le-raglaw ("to its feet/legs"); the anatomical description is of the locust-type insect: it has jointed rear legs with a prominent upper section ("shanks above the feet") — the two-segmented jumping legs of orthoptera (grasshoppers, locusts, crickets); the LXX renders lo' kera'ayim mi-ma'al le-raglaw as οἷς ἐστιν σκέλη ἀνώτερον τῶν ποδῶν αὐτοῦ (hois estin skelē anōteron tōn podōn autou, "which has legs higher than its feet"), using σκέλη (skelē, "legs") and ἀνώτερον (anōteron, "higher") — preserving the anatomical sense], to leap upon the earth with them [le-natter bahen al-ha-arets] [le-natter bahen al-ha-arets — "to spring/leap with them upon the earth"; natter is the hiphil infinitive of natar, "to spring, leap, jump"; this confirms the anatomical description: the legs described in the first clause are the jumping legs; the creature uses them to leap; the LXX renders le-natter as πηδᾶν (pēdan, "to leap / to jump") — accurate]:

**22** "These [eleh] [are] them you shall eat [min-hem tokhelu] from them: the locust [ha-arbeh] after its kind [le-mino] [ha-arbeh le-mino — "the locust after its kind"; arbeh is the migratory locust (Locusta migratoria), the most destructive insect plague in the ancient Near East; it is the primary subject of Ex 10:4-15 and Joel 1-2; le-mino extends the permission to all locust species; the LXX renders arbeh as ἀκρίδα (akrida, "locust"), which is the term used in Matt 3:4 and Mark 1:6 for the food of John the Baptist], and the locust-variant [ve-ha-sal'am] le-mino [ve-ha-sal'am le-mino — "and the sal'am after its kind"; sal'am is an unidentified orthopteran, possibly the desert locust (Schistocerca gregaria) or a large grasshopper species; hapax legomenon in this list; LXX: ἀττάκην (attakēn, "attakes" — a large grasshopper species)], and the cricket [ve-ha-chargol] le-mino [ve-ha-chargol le-mino — "and the cricket after its kind"; chargol is likely a cricket or smaller grasshopper; some identify it with a cricket species (Gryllus); LXX: ὀφιομάχην (ophiomachēn, "serpent-fighter" — a name for a type of locust that supposedly fights snakes) — an uncertain and possibly folkloric identification], and the grasshopper [ve-ha-chagav] le-mino [ve-ha-chagav le-mino — "and the grasshopper/locust after its kind"; chagav is used in Num 13:33, where the spies say "we were in our own eyes as grasshoppers" (kachagavim) — indicating a small grasshopper species; in Eccl 12:5 it appears in the context of aging; LXX: βροῦχον (brouchon, "young locust / locust-nymph")].

**23** "And every [other] swarming-thing of the birds [u-khol sherets ha-'of] that has four legs [asher lo arba' raglayim] — it is a detestable-thing to you [sheqets hu' lakhem]."

---

**24** "And by these [u-le-eleh] [u-le-eleh — "and by these"; the preposition le is here instrumental or causal: "through these / on account of these"; it introduces the new section on carcass-defilement — the objects of the following verses are the carcasses of the animals just classified, and the defilement comes through contact with them] you will be made-unclean [tittame'u] [tittame'u — 2nd person plural niphal of tame', "to be made unclean / to become ritually impure"; the niphal here is tolerative or resultative: contact with a carcass produces a state of tum'ah (ritual impurity) in the person who touches it; the impurity is conveyed, not merely incurred — the person enters a new state that requires resolution (washing, waiting until evening)]: everyone who touches [kol-ha-nogea'] their carcass [be-nivlatam] shall be unclean until the evening [yitma' ad-ha-arev] [yitma' ad-ha-arev — "shall be unclean until the evening"; the period of impurity for carcass-contact is one day — from the moment of contact until sundown; this is the lightest degree of impurity in the Levitical system, resolved by the sunset alone (without requiring a sin-offering, unlike more severe impurities); the LXX renders yitma' ad-ha-arev as ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ἕως ἑσπέρας (akathartos estai heōs hesperas, "shall be unclean until evening")]:

**25** "And everyone who carries [ve-kol-ha-nose'] from their carcass [mi-nivlatam] shall wash his garments [yekhabbes begadav] [yekhabbes begadav — "shall wash his garments"; kibbes (piel of kabas) is the term for washing fabric — a more intensive cleansing than rachats (washing flesh/body); the distinction is maintained throughout Leviticus: body-washing uses rachats; garment-washing uses kibbes; carrying (as opposed to merely touching) a carcass requires the additional step of garment-washing, reflecting a higher degree of contamination through more sustained or absorbed contact] and shall be unclean until the evening [ve-tame' ad-ha-arev]:

**26** "For every animal [le-khol-ha-behemah] that divides the hoof [mafresset parsah] but is not cleaving-a-cleft [ve-shesa' eynah shosaat] [ve-shesa' eynah shosaat — "and a cleavage it is not cleaving"; i.e., the hoof appears divided but is not fully cloven; this may refer to animals with a partial hoof-division that resembles but does not meet the full criterion of v.3; the donkey, horse, and camel are often cited; the phrase mirrors the positive criterion of v.3 in its negative form] and the cud does not bring up [ve-gerah eynah ma'alah] — they are unclean for you [teme'im hem lakhem]; everyone who touches them [kol-ha-nogea' bahem] shall be unclean [yitma']:

**27** "And everything that walks on its paws [ve-khol holek al-kappav] [ve-khol holek al-kappav — "and everything walking on its paws"; kappav is the plural of kaph with pronominal suffix: "its paws / its palms / its hands"; kaph is the flat of the hand or foot, as opposed to the hoof (parsah) or the sole (kaf ha-regel); the paw-walking animals are the plantigrade carnivores — bears, lions, dogs, cats — as well as many other mammals that walk on the palms of their feet rather than on hooves; the LXX renders al-kappav as ἐπὶ τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ (epi tous podas autou, "on its feet") — a general rendering that loses the specific "paw/palm" nuance of kaph] among all the animals [ba-kol-ha-chayyah] — they are unclean to you [hamma hem lakhem] [hamma hem lakhem — "they are unclean for you"; hamma is likely a scribal/textual variant or dialectal form; the Leningrad Codex reads hamma (הֵמָּה, "they") rather than the expected teme'im ("unclean"); the clause reads "they — [are unclean] — for you" with the adjective understood from context; the LXX reads ταῦτα ἀκάθαρτα ὑμῖν (tauta akathartha hymin, "these [are] unclean for you"), supplying ἀκάθαρτα explicitly]; everyone who touches their carcass [kol-ha-nogea' be-nivlatam] shall be unclean until the evening [yitma' ad-ha-arev]:

**28** "And one who carries [ve-ha-nose'] their carcass [et-nivlatam] shall wash his garments [yekhabbes et-begadav] and shall be unclean until the evening [ve-tame' ad-ha-arev] — for they are unclean to you [ki teme'im hem lakhem]."

---

**29** "And this [is] the unclean [one] [ve-zeh lakhem ha-tame'] for you [lakhem] among the swarming-things [ba-sheresets] [ba-sheresets — "among the swarming-things"; the sherets category here is the land-swarming category, distinct from the water-swarmers of vv.10-12 and the flying-swarmers of vv.20-23; the land-swarmers include small reptiles, rodents, and invertebrates that "swarm" (sharats = move in great numbers, prolifically) on the ground; the following list of eight animals gives specific exemplars from this category] that swarm [ha-shorets] upon the earth [al-ha-arets]: the mole [ha-choled] [ha-choled — "the mole / the weasel"; choled is most likely the weasel (Mustela nivalis) or the mole-rat (Spalax ehrenbergi), both common in the Levant; the weasel identification is supported by the LXX: γαλῆν (galēn, "weasel")], and the mouse [ve-ha-'akhbar] [ve-ha-'akhbar — "and the mouse"; 'akhbar is the house mouse (Mus musculus) or a related murid rodent; it also appears in the plague narrative of 1 Sam 6:4-5 as the source of land-infestation; LXX: μῦν (myn, "mouse") — accurate], and the great-lizard [ve-ha-tsav] after its kind [le-minehu] [ve-ha-tsav le-minehu — "and the great-lizard / the toad after its kind"; tsav is uncertain; it may be the dabb lizard (Uromastyx ornata), a large spiny-tailed lizard common in the Middle East; others suggest a tortoise or toad; le-minehu extends the prohibition to all species of this type; LXX: χερσαῖον τὸν κροκόδειλον (chersaion ton krokodeilon, "the land-crocodile") — suggesting a large lizard]:

**30** "And the gecko [ve-ha-ana'qah] [ve-ha-ana'qah — "and the gecko / the ferret"; ana'qah may derive from ana'q ("to cry, groan") — a creature with a distinctive cry or call; most likely the tokay gecko (Gekko gecko) or the fan-footed gecko (Ptyodactylus hasselquistii), whose calls are audible; LXX: μυγαλῆν (mygalēn, "field-mouse / shrew")], and the monitor-lizard [ve-ha-koach] [ve-ha-koach — "and the monitor-lizard / the land-crocodile"; koach literally means "strength / power"; as a creature name it likely refers to the Nile monitor lizard (Varanus niloticus) or the desert monitor (Varanus griseus), the largest lizards of the Levant; LXX: χαμαιλέοντα (chamaileonta, "chameleon") — an identification disputed by most modern scholars], and the gecko-lizard [ve-ha-leta'ah] [ve-ha-leta'ah — "and the gecko / the lizard"; leta'ah from lata' ("to swallow"?) — uncertain; likely a gecko or skink species; LXX: σαύραν (sauran, "lizard") — general identification], and the skink [ve-ha-chomet] [ve-ha-chomet — "and the skink / the sand-lizard"; chomet is uncertain; some propose the garden skink (Scincus scincus); others a sand-lizard; LXX: σαύρᾳ (saura, "lizard")], and the chameleon [ve-ha-tinshemet] [ve-ha-tinshemet — "and the chameleon"; here tinshemet appears in the reptile list and is likely the chameleon (Chamaeleo chamaeleon), distinct from its use in the bird-list at v.18 (where it may mean "barn-owl"); the same Hebrew word appears to cover two different animals in the two lists — a recognized ambiguity in the Levitical text; LXX: ἀσπάλακα (aspalaka, "mole")]:

**31** "These are the unclean for you [eleh ha-teme'im lakhem] among all the swarming-things [be-kol-ha-sheresets] — everyone who touches them [kol-ha-nogea' bahem] at their death [be-motam] [be-motam — "in their death / when they are dead"; the defilement from the dead land-swarmers is specifically post-mortem: contact with a living lizard or mouse does not convey tum'ah; it is the carcass (nivlah, the dead body) that contaminates; this detail is important for the following agricultural/vessel rules (vv.32-38)] shall be unclean until the evening [yitma' ad-ha-arev]:

**32** "And everything upon which any of them [falls] in their death [ve-kol asher yippol alav mehem be-motam] [ve-kol asher yippol alav mehem be-motam — "and everything upon which any of them falls in their death / when they die"; the verse shifts from person-contact to object-contamination: when a dead swarming creature falls onto an object, the object becomes unclean; this is the principle of secondary contamination through contact with a contaminating source] shall be made-unclean [yitma'] — from any vessel of wood [mi-kol-keli-'ets] [mi-kol-keli-'ets — "from any vessel of wood"; the following list of vessel materials (wood, cloth, skin, sackcloth, wood-again, or anything used for work) covers the major categories of household objects and manufacturing materials; the principle is that porous or absorbent materials convey impurity through contact; the LXX renders keli-'ets as σκεῦος ξύλινον (skeuos xylinos, "wooden vessel"), using σκεῦος (skeuos, "vessel, implement, container") — the same term used in NT for vessels of various kinds (Rom 9:21-23; 2 Tim 2:20-21)] or garment-cloth [o beged] or skin [o-'or] or sackcloth [o saq] [o saq — "or sackcloth"; saq is coarse woven material made from goat or camel hair, used for mourning garments (Gen 37:34; 2 Sam 3:31; Joel 1:8) and for grain-storage bags (Gen 42:25-27); it is a distinct material category from beged (finely woven cloth) and 'or (leather/skin)]; any vessel [kol-keli] with which work is done [asher ye'aseh melakhah bahem] [asher ye'aseh melakhah bahem — "with which work is done in them / by which work is performed"; the criterion is functional utility — an object used for any kind of work is subject to the contamination rule; discarded or non-functional objects may not fall under the rule (cf. the rule of non-vessels in later halakha); LXX: πᾶν σκεῦος, ὃ ἂν ποιηθῇ ἐν αὐτοῖς ἔργον (pan skeuos ho an poiēthē en autois ergon, "any vessel in which work may be done")] shall be brought [yuva'] into the water [ba-mayim] [ba-mayim — "into the water"; immersion in water (tevilah) is the resolution for vessel-contamination; the affected vessel is submerged in water; if the vessel is ceramic (earthenware), the rule is different — it must be broken (v.33)]; and it shall be unclean until the evening [ve-tame' ad-ha-arev] — and it will be clean [ve-taher] [ve-taher — "and it will be clean"; after immersion and the waiting-period until evening, the vessel returns to a state of tahor (cleanness); the pattern — impurity contracted, water-immersion, evening-waiting, cleanness restored — is the paradigmatic Levitical purification sequence, elaborated and systematized in the later tractates of the Mishnah (especially Kelim, Mikva'ot, Tevul Yom)]:

**33** "And every earthenware vessel [ve-khol-keli-cheres] [ve-khol-keli-cheres — "and every vessel of pottery / earthenware"; cheres is baked clay pottery, as distinct from 'ets (wood), 'or (leather), and metal vessels; the rule for earthenware is more severe than for other vessels: it cannot be purified by immersion but must be broken, presumably because its porous unglazed interior absorbs the impurity and cannot be cleansed by surface washing; this rule for pottery contamination is developed at length in Mishnah Kelim] into whose interior [asher yippol me-hem el-tokho] [asher yippol me-hem el-tokho — "into whose interior any of them falls"; el-tokho = "into its interior / into its inside"; the contamination of pottery is through interior contact — the falling of the dead swarmer into the interior of the pot; an earthenware vessel contaminated on the outside (exterior surface) follows different rules in later halakha; the LXX renders el-tokho as εἰς αὐτό (eis auto, "into it")] any of them falls [yippol] — everything in its interior [kol asher be-tokho] shall be unclean [yitma'] and it [itself] [ve-oto] you shall break [tishboru] [tishboru — "you shall break it"; the imperative to destroy a contaminated earthenware vessel is unambiguous; the verb shabar means "to break, to shatter"; the vessel is not merely set aside but destroyed; this reflects the principle that fired clay absorbs impurity that cannot be expelled by washing]:

**34** "Of all the food [mi-kol-ha-okhel] [mi-kol-ha-okhel — "from all the food"; okhel is food in general; v.34 introduces the contamination of food and drink by contact with contaminated water, extending the principle of secondary-contamination from vessels to foodstuffs] which may be eaten [asher ye'akhel] upon which water comes [asher yavo' alav mayim] [asher yavo' alav mayim — "upon which water comes / onto which water is poured"; the food must have been wetted — either by human intention or by accident — before it is susceptible to food-contamination; this rule of hakhsharah ("readying / preparing food for contamination") is a fundamental principle: dry foods cannot contract impurity; only foods that have been wetted are susceptible; the Mishnah (Machshirin) devotes an entire tractate to the seven liquids that "ready" food for impurity: water, dew, oil, wine, milk, blood, and bee-honey; the verse uses only water as the paradigmatic example] — shall be unclean [yitma'] [the rule: a clean vessel of water contaminated by a dead swarmer (vv.29-31) can then transfer contamination to any food it contacts, rendering the food unclean]; and all drink [ve-khol-mashqeh] that may be drunk [asher yishteh] in every [such] vessel [be-khol-keli] shall be unclean [yitma']:

**35** "And everything upon which [any part of] their carcass falls [ve-kol asher yippol mi-nivlatam alav] — it shall be unclean [yitma']; an oven [ha-tannur] [ha-tannur — "the oven / the clay oven / the tannur"; the tannur is a clay cylindrical oven in which bread is baked by pressing dough to the heated inner walls; it is a common household item throughout the ancient Near East and is still in use today as the tandoor; because it is made of clay (cheres, earthenware) it follows the earthenware-breaking rule; LXX: κλίβανος (klibanos, "clay-oven")] or a cooking-range [ve-kirayim] [ve-kirayim — "and cooking-ranges / double-burner stoves"; kirayim is the dual form of kir ("furnace / kiln / cooking apparatus"), suggesting a double cooking surface or double-grate; it is a clay cooking apparatus, thus also subject to the earthenware-breaking rule; LXX: χυτρόπους (chytropous, "pot-stand / trivet")] shall be broken-down [yittats] [yittats — niphal of natats, "to break down, to tear down, to demolish"; a stronger verb than shabar (v.33); the oven and cooking-range, being larger structures, are not merely broken (shabar) but torn down/demolished (natats); the LXX renders yittats as καταλυθήσεται (katalythēsetai, "shall be dissolved / shall be torn down") — accurate]; they are unclean [teme'im hem] and shall be unclean to you [u-teme'im yihyu lakhem]:

**36** "Nevertheless [akh] — a spring [ma'ayan] [ma'ayan — "a spring / an eye of water"; ma'ayan (from 'ayin, "eye") is a natural spring; the flowing water of a spring is qualitatively different from still water in a vessel — it is self-replenishing and thus cannot become permanently contaminated; this is the principle behind the later rabbinic classification of "living water" (mayim chayyim, lit. "living/flowing waters") as the most effective purification medium and as inherently resistant to impurity] or a pit [ve-bor] [ve-bor — "and a cistern / a pit"; a bor is a stone-lined cistern cut into the rock, used for collecting rainwater; it is distinguished from a ma'ayan (natural spring) in that it contains stored rather than flowing water; the inclusion of both ma'ayan and bor signals that underground/contained large water stores resist contamination in a way that smaller household vessels do not] — an assembly of water [miqveh mayim] [miqveh mayim — "a collection/gathering of waters / a reservoir"; miqveh is the noun from the verb qavah ("to gather, to hope, to wait"); it means "gathering, collection" — specifically a collection of water; this is the term from which the rabbinic institution of the mikveh (ritual immersion bath) derives its name; the technical requirements for a valid mikveh (minimum 40 seahs of "gathered water," natural connection to the ground, etc.) are elaborated in Mishnah Mikva'ot, which cites this verse as its foundational scriptural authority] shall be clean [tahor yihyeh] [tahor yihyeh — "shall be clean / shall remain clean"; a large natural water supply is not rendered unclean by a dead swarmer falling into it; this exempts wells, cisterns, and springs from the contamination-rules of v.33-35]; but [what touches] their carcass shall be unclean [ve-nogea' be-nivlatam yitma']:

**37** "And if [any part of] their carcass falls [ve-ki yippol mi-nivlatam] upon any seed-grain [al-kol-zera' zeru'a] [al-kol-zera' zeru'a — "upon any sown seed / seed for sowing"; zera' is seed, specifically grain-seed ready for planting; zeru'a is the qal passive participle, "that which is sown"; together: "seed prepared for sowing"; the rule is: dry unplanted seed is NOT contaminated by contact with a dead swarmer — the seed retains its clean status because it is dry and not yet wetted/softened] which is to be sown [asher yizare'a] — it is clean [tahor hu']:

**38** "But if water is put [ve-ki yuttan mayim] [ve-ki yuttan mayim — "but if water is placed / poured"; the passive of natan ("to give/place"); this is the hakhsharah moment — once water is applied to the seed, the seed becomes susceptible to contamination; the deliberate act of wetting changes the seed's ritual status from invulnerable to vulnerable] upon the seed [al-ha-zera'] and [any part of] their carcass falls [ve-naphals mi-nivlatam] upon it [alav] — it is unclean [tame' hu'] to you [lakhem]."

---

**39** "And if any of the animals [ve-ki yamut min-ha-behemah] [ve-ki yamut min-ha-behemah — "and if any of the clean animals dies"; yamut is qal of mut, "to die"; the subject is an animal from the behemah class — specifically a clean animal (one that meets the hoof-and-cud criteria) that has died of natural causes rather than being properly slaughtered; the following rules govern contact with the carcass of a clean animal that has not been ritually slaughtered] that is clean for you [asher hi' lakhem le-okhlah] [asher hi' lakhem le-okhlah — "which is for you for food / which you [may] eat"; this clause identifies the animal in question as a clean (edible) species; if it dies without slaughter, it becomes a neveilah ("carcass," an animal that died of itself rather than through slaughter) and is prohibited for eating (Lev 17:15; Deut 14:21); but the defilement-rules for contacting such a carcass still apply] — dies [yamut]: he who touches [ha-nogea'] its carcass [be-nivlatah] shall be unclean until the evening [yitma' ad-ha-arev]:

**40** "And he who eats [ve-ha-okhel] from its carcass [mi-nivlatah] shall wash his garments [yekhabbes begadav] and shall be unclean until the evening [ve-tame' ad-ha-arev]; and he who carries [ve-ha-nose'] its carcass [et-nivlatah] shall wash his garments [yekhabbes begadav] and shall be unclean until the evening [ve-tame' ad-ha-arev] [the three levels of contact with a clean animal's carcass — touching, eating, carrying — produce the same degree of impurity (tame' until evening) but with different resolution requirements: touching requires only the waiting-until-evening (v.39); eating and carrying additionally require garment-washing (v.40); this gradation — lesser contact, lesser remediation — is consistent with the principle throughout the chapter that the degree of remediation tracks the degree of contact]."

---

**41** "And every swarming-thing [ve-khol-ha-sheresets] that swarms [ha-shorets] upon the earth [al-ha-arets] — it is a detestable-thing [sheqets hu'] — it shall not be eaten [lo' ye'akhel]:

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex B19A, Folios 63v–64r)

1. **v.1 — Double address to Moshe and Aharon:** The opening address vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe ve-el-Aharon ("and the LORD spoke to Moshe and to Aharon") is unique within the Levitical dietary legislation. In the broader context of Leviticus 1–10, divine speech is addressed to Moshe alone (Lev 1:1; 4:1; 5:14; 6:1, 8, 19, 24; 7:22, 28; 8:1) or, once, to Aharon alone (10:8). The dual address of 11:1 (repeated at 13:1; 14:33; 15:1) may reflect the teaching function of this material: the dietary laws are given both to be transmitted through the Mosaic legislative channel and to be taught and administered by the Aaronide priesthood (cf. 10:10-11, where Aharon is specifically commissioned to teach the distinction between clean and unclean). The Samaritan Pentateuch agrees with the MT on the dual address. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων (kai elalēsen kyrios pros Mōusēn kai Aarōn), confirming the MT.

2. **vv.4-8 — The four case-study animals and their pedagogical function:** The structure of vv.4-8 is carefully constructed: three animals that chew cud but lack split hooves (camel, rock-badger, hare) + one animal that splits the hoof but does not chew cud (pig). This is not a random zoological survey but a set of paradigmatic test-cases: the text teaches the dual-criterion rule by application, showing what happens when an animal meets one criterion but not the other. The pedagogical strategy is important — by working through the borderline cases, the text trains the reader in the principle before applying it to the full range of animals. The pig is listed last and described with the most elaborate hoof-language (mafris parsah AND shosa' shesa' parsah — both hoof-criteria phrases applied) precisely to make the contrast with the cud-failure maximally stark. This structural emphasis on the pig (meeting the visible external criterion while failing the invisible internal criterion) has generated enormous secondary tradition in Jewish hermeneutics — the pig becomes the emblem of hypocritical holiness, of external marks without internal reality. The Midrash (Vayikra Rabbah 13:5) develops this symbolically: Rome is the "pig among the nations" — displaying the split hoof of external legal justice while lacking the cud-chewing of internal righteousness.

3. **vv.10, 12 — Sheqets vs. tame':** The chapter uses two distinct terms for prohibited status: tame' ("unclean," vv.4-8, 24-40) and sheqets ("detestable-thing / abomination," vv.10-12, 13, 20, 23, 41-42). The distinction is significant and is not interchangeable in the MT's usage. Tame' (from a root meaning "to be defiled") is the standard ritual-impurity term — it describes a state that can be contracted, transmitted, and resolved through purification procedures. Sheqets (from shaqats, "to abhor, to detest") is a stronger evaluative term describing ritual-revulsion — it characterizes the object as categorically abhorrent, such that the appropriate response is not merely avoidance but active disgust. Land animals that fail the criteria (vv.4-8) are called tame' — their carcasses convey impurity, but the prohibition is relatively neutral. Water creatures (vv.10-12) and flying insects (vv.20-23) are called sheqets — they are positively abhorrent. The land-swarmers (vv.41-43) are called both sheqets and (implicitly) tame' — they carry both the abomination-characterization and the impurity-conveying quality. The LXX renders both terms fairly consistently: tame' as ἀκάθαρτος (akathartos), sheqets as βδέλυγμα (bdelygma).

4. **v.36 — Ma'ayan u-vor miqveh mayim / The purity of gathered waters:** The ruling of v.36 — that a spring (ma'ayan) or a cistern-reservoir (bor miqveh mayim) is not contaminated by a dead swarmer — is the biblical foundation for the entire rabbinic institution of the mikveh (ritual immersion pool). The term miqveh mayim ("a gathering/collection of waters") from this verse provides the technical name and the legal basis. Mishnah Mikva'ot (the tractate on immersion pools) begins from this verse and develops elaborate criteria: a kosher mikveh must hold at least 40 seahs (approximately 200 gallons / 750 liters) of naturally gathered water (mayim she'uvim, "drawn water," is invalid for a mikveh), must be connected to the ground, and may not be drawn off into containers (which would make it subject to the vessel-contamination rules of v.33). The distinction between the large natural gathering of water (tahor — clean, resistant to contamination) and the small individual vessel (tame' when contaminated) drives the entire logic of immersion-purification: to purify oneself from impurity, one immerses in a body of water large enough to qualify as a miqveh — water in the category that resists contamination rather than transmits it. The Leningrad Codex text of v.36 is clear and well-preserved on folio 64r.

5. **v.37-38 — Hakhsharah / The readying of food for contamination:** The principle articulated in vv.37-38 — that dry seed is immune from contamination but wet seed is susceptible — establishes the foundational rule of hakhsharah ("readying / preparing [for contamination]"), which the Mishnah develops into a full tractate (Machshirin, "Preparers"). The seven liquids that render food susceptible to contamination (water, dew, oil, wine, milk, blood, bee-honey) are derived from the hint of v.34 (mayim, "water") and developed through Levitical and rabbinic exegesis. The practical logic of the rule is elegant: a dry grain heap cannot be contaminated by a dead mouse falling into it; but once water is added (for soaking prior to grinding), the grain enters a contamination-susceptible state. The rule thus creates a practical window for grain commerce (dry grain can be handled without worry) while maintaining the purity framework for food once processing begins.

6. **vv.44-45 — "Be holy, for I am holy" — The theological keystone:** The dual qedushah-formula of vv.44 and 45 is arguably the most theologically dense statement in all of Leviticus and one of the central verses of the entire Torah. Its theological logic is:

    - Divine indicative: "I am the LORD your God" (covenant self-identification)
    - Divine historical act: "who brought you up from the land of Egypt" (Exodus as covenant-founding event)
    - Divine purposive: "to be your God" (the Exodus was for the purpose of covenantal relationship)
    - Divine ontological declaration: "for I am holy" (the LORD's own nature is qadosh — set apart, other, categorically distinct)
    - Human imperative derived from divine nature: "you shall be holy" (Israel's holiness is a participation in and response to the divine holiness)
    - Human imperative applied concretely: "do not make yourselves unclean with every swarming thing that creeps on the earth" (the abstract call to holiness is cashed out in the specific practice of dietary distinction)

    The connection between divine holiness and food-law obedience is not merely rhetorical — it is ontological in the Levitical framework. The LORD's qedushah means his categorical distinctness, his absolute separation from all that is common, chaotic, or degraded. Israel's qedushah means Israel's corresponding distinctness — a people set apart from the nations (Lev 20:24-26 makes this explicit: "I have separated you from the peoples... you shall be holy to me, for I the LORD am holy and have separated you from the peoples to be mine"). The dietary laws enact this separation at the most basic level of bodily life: what the Israelite puts in the body reflects and reinforces the categorical identity of Israel as a people belonging to the holy God.

    The NT citation of this verse in 1 Peter 1:15-16 applies it to Christian ethical holiness ("as the one who called you is holy, you also be holy in all your conduct"), detaching it from the specific food-law context while preserving its theological structure: divine holiness → covenantal calling → human holiness-imperative. Peter's application implies that the dietary laws functioned in Israel as concrete expressions of the holiness-principle, and that the holiness-principle itself remains normative even when its dietary-law expressions are fulfilled or transcended in the new covenant.

7. **v.47 — Le-havdil / "To distinguish" as the theological summary-word:** The closing word le-havdil ("to distinguish/separate") positions the entire dietary legislation as an exercise in the fundamental priestly and divine activity of distinction-making. The verb havdil (hiphil of badal, "to separate") has appeared at three key junctures:
    - Gen 1:4, 6, 7, 14, 18: God separates (va-yavdel) light from darkness, waters from waters, day from night — the acts of creation are acts of distinction-making
    - Lev 10:10: The priest is commissioned to distinguish (le-havdil) between holy and common, clean and unclean — the acts of priestly vocation are acts of distinction-making
    - Lev 11:47: The dietary laws conclude with le-havdil — the acts of daily eating are acts of distinction-making

    The threefold occurrence of havdil at these three junctures (creation / priestly vocation / daily eating) is not coincidental. The dietary laws embed the Israelite in a daily practice that mirrors both the divine creative activity and the priestly sanctuary-activity. Each meal is an act of participation in the cosmic order established by God's original separations. The Havdalah ceremony of post-Sabbath Judaism (also from the root havdal) continues this theological logic: the end of the Sabbath is marked by a ceremony of distinction (between holy and common, light and darkness, Israel and the nations, Sabbath and weekdays) that mirrors the Levitical distinction-making imperative. The chapter that begins with dietary specifics ends with a theology of distinction that encompasses creation, covenant, and daily life.

### LXX (Codex Vaticanus) Observations — Pages 95–96

The LXX of Leviticus 11 presents numerous identification challenges, particularly in the bird-list (vv.13-19) and reptile-list (vv.29-30), where the Greek translators clearly struggled with identifying several animals that were unfamiliar in the Alexandrian Egyptian context. The following are the most significant divergences:

- **vv.5-6 — Shaphan / χοιρογρύλλιον and arnevet / δασύπους:** The LXX renders shaphan (rock-badger/hyrax) as χοιρογρύλλιον (choirogyllion, "pig-squealer" — an undefined creature) and arnevet (hare) as δασύπους (dasypous, "hairy/rough-footed"). The choirogyllion identification for shaphan is puzzling — the LXX translators may have been uncertain what a shaphan was and described it by some characteristic sound or appearance; the term does not correspond to any identifiable animal in Greek zoological tradition. The dasypous for arnevet ("hairy-footed one") is actually a reasonable description of the hare's padded feet, and dasypous became the standard Greek word for "hare" in later literature.

- **v.13 — Nesher / ἀετόν:** The LXX renders nesher as ἀετόν (eagle). As noted in the translation, nesher probably more precisely refers to the griffon vulture in many passages. However, the eagle identification is ancient and consistent across the LXX, Vulgate, and most early versions. The identification matters because the eagle is a royal and even positive symbol in many ancient cultures (including Israelite: Ex 19:4; Deut 32:11; Ps 103:5; Isa 40:31), while the vulture is exclusively associated with carrion; the choice of nesher as the chapter's lead prohibited bird may thus be rhetorically significant regardless of exact species.

- **v.17 — Yanshuf / ἴβιν:** The LXX's identification of yanshuf as ἴβιν (ibis) is one of the most striking divergences in the chapter. The ibis (Threskiornis aethiopicus) was a sacred bird of Egypt, associated with the god Thoth, protected by law, and absolutely central to Egyptian religious practice. The LXX's identification of yanshuf with the ibis may reflect a deliberately ironic anti-Egyptian polemic: a bird that the Egyptians worship as divine and hold sacred is, in the Levitical system, a prohibited, unclean bird. If the LXX translators intended this identification, it would represent a pointed theological statement embedded in the translation. However, it is also possible that the identification is simply erroneous — the translators may not have known what yanshuf was and identified it with an Egyptian bird known to them.

- **vv.29-30 — The land-swarmers list:** The LXX translations of the eight land-swarming creatures (vv.29-30) are notably divergent from modern identifications and from each other across manuscript traditions. The Vaticanus reading includes: γαλῆ (galē, "weasel") for choled; μῦς (mys, "mouse") for 'akhbar; χερσαῖος κροκόδειλος (chersaios krokodeilos, "land-crocodile") for tsav; μυγαλῆ (mygalē, "field-mouse/shrew") for ana'qah; χαμαιλέων (chamaileōn, "chameleon") for koach; σαύρα (saura, "lizard") for both leta'ah and chomet; ἀσπάλαξ (aspalax, "mole") for tinshemet. The identification of koach as "chameleon" (when the word literally means "strength" and probably refers to the monitor lizard) and tinshemet as "mole" (when the same word in v.18 the LXX renders differently) indicate that the LXX translators were working with genuine uncertainty about several of these species. The Alexandrian Jewish community may not have been familiar with all the small reptiles and mammals of the Levant, leading to guesses based on general appearance or folk-equivalences.

- **v.36 — Miqveh mayim / συναγωγὴ ὕδατος:** The LXX renders miqveh mayim as συναγωγὴ ὕδατος (synagōgē hydatos, "a gathering/assembly of water"), using συναγωγή (synagōgē, "gathering, assembly") — the same word that gives us "synagogue." The LXX thus renders the founding term of the mikveh institution with the word that will later designate the primary Jewish communal institution. The etymological connection is real: synagōgē and miqveh both mean "gathering/collection" (synagōgē from synagō, "to bring together"; miqveh from qavah/qivvah, "to gather"). The coincidence of vocabulary highlights the deep connection between the gathering of water (the mikveh) and the gathering of the community (the synagogue) in later Jewish life — both are expressions of the same root-concept of purposive gathering.

- **v.44 — Ve-hitqadishtem / ἅγιοι ἔσεσθε:** The LXX renders the hithpael reflexive ve-hitqadishtem ("and you shall make yourselves holy / consecrate yourselves") as the simple adjective + future-of-to-be: ἅγιοι ἔσεσθε ("and you shall be holy"), losing the reflexive-active dimension of the Hebrew. The difference is subtle but theologically meaningful: the MT's hithpael implies that holiness is something Israel actively works toward through its practices (including the dietary laws just given); the LXX's ἅγιοι ἔσεσθε implies a state to be inhabited. Both are defensible readings of the holiness-imperative, but the MT's reflexive form gives the dietary laws a more direct role in the production of holiness — they are not merely markers of an already-given holy status but means by which the Israelite actively consecrates (makes-holy) himself or herself. First Peter 1:15 follows neither the MT nor the LXX precisely: γένητε ἅγιοι (genēthe hagioi, "become holy") — using the aorist imperative of γίνομαι (ginomai, "to become"), which captures the MT's active/reflexive sense better than the LXX's simple future.
